questions.q: What is the main difference between a queue and a pub/sub topic?
questions.a: A queue delivers each message to one consumer, while pub/sub fan-outs to many subscribers.

questions.q: What does ACK mean in a brokered message system?
questions.a: It confirms that a consumer processed or accepted the message.
A queue for messages that could not be processed successfully after retries.
A set of consumers that share work across partitions or messages.

questions.q: What is a partition in a log-based broker?
questions.a: An ordered shard of a topic that can be consumed independently.

questions.q: What does offset mean in Kafka-like systems?
questions.a: The position of a message within a partition.

questions.q: Why do message brokers often provide at-least-once delivery by default?
questions.a: Because retries after failures are safer than losing messages.

questions.q: Why is exactly-once delivery hard to guarantee?
questions.a: Failures can happen between producing, consuming, and committing state.
Because duplicate deliveries can happen during retries or rebalances.

questions.q: Why is ordering easier within one partition than across many?
questions.a: A single partition has one total sequence, while many partitions do not.

questions.q: What is backpressure in queue systems?
questions.a: It is a signal to slow producers or reduce intake when consumers lag.

questions.q: Why do poison messages need special handling?
questions.a: They fail repeatedly and can block throughput if they are retried forever.

questions.q: When would you choose a queue over a direct synchronous API call?
questions.a: When you want buffering, retry isolation, and asynchronous processing.

questions.q: When would a log-based broker be a better fit than a classic queue?
questions.a: When you need replay, multiple consumers, and durable event history.

questions.q: How would you design around duplicate deliveries?
questions.a: Make handlers idempotent, dedupe by message ID, and commit side effects carefully.

questions.q: When is a DLQ better than infinite retries?
questions.a: When a message is invalid or depends on a fix that cannot be made automatically.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with ordering guarantees?
questions.a: Stronger ordering usually reduces parallelism and can lower throughput.
When throughput matters more than per-message latency and the broker supports it.
